title: BikeWatch
description: An interactive map visualization of bike traffic in the Boston area using BlueBike data.
story: I wanted to understand how people move through a city on bikes. Combining Mapbox, D3, and real BlueBike trip data turned out to be more interesting than I expected. Watching the circles change color and size as you scrub through different times of day made the data feel alive in a way that a spreadsheet never could.

title: Personal Portfolio Website
description: A personal website built with SvelteKit showcasing my projects and interests.
story: I wanted a place on the internet that felt like mine. Building it taught me more about web development than any tutorial could. Every small decision about layout and color forced me to think about what I actually wanted to say about myself.

title: GitHub Stats Dashboard
description: A live dashboard pulling from the GitHub API to display repository and profile stats.
story: APIs always felt abstract until I built something that actually depended on one. Watching real data appear on screen for the first time made everything click. I started thinking of the web less as pages and more as a conversation between services.

title: Sudoku Solver
description: A backtracking algorithm that solves any valid Sudoku puzzle.
story: I play Sudoku to relax, which made it funny when writing a solver turned into one of the most frustrating debugging sessions of my life. The backtracking algorithm is elegant in theory and humbling in practice.

title: Restaurant Roulette
description: A Chicago restaurant recommender that filters by neighborhood and cuisine.
story: Growing up in Chicago means having strong opinions about food. I built this to settle arguments about where to eat, and ended up learning more about the city's neighborhoods than I expected. Every dataset tells a story if you look closely enough.

title: Math Notes Visualizer
description: An interactive tool for visualizing linear algebra concepts like eigenvectors and transformations.
story: Linear algebra made no sense to me until I could see it. I built small visualizations just for myself at first, to check my own intuition. Eventually they became something I shared with classmates, and watching them have the same lightbulb moments made it worthwhile.

title: Workout Tracker
description: A minimal app to log gym sessions and track progress over time.
story: I got tired of spreadsheets. I wanted something that matched how I actually think about training, which a sense of momentum over time. Building it forced me to think hard about what data actually matters and what's just noise.

title: Campus Event Finder
description: A scraper and aggregator for MIT campus events, filtered by interest.
story: MIT has hundreds of events happening at any given time, and finding them felt like a full-time job. I built a scraper partly out of frustration and partly to learn how web scraping works. The hardest part wasn't the code, but it was deciding what counted as interesting.

title: Chicago Food Desert Map
description: A data visualization mapping grocery store access across Chicago neighborhoods.
story: I grew up in Chicago but never really thought about who had access to fresh food until I saw the data. Some neighborhoods I knew well looked completely different through the lens of a map. This project changed how I think about cities and what it means to grow up in one place versus another.

title: Pomodoro Timer
description: A minimal productivity timer built around the Pomodoro technique, with session tracking.
story: Freshman year I was convinced my productivity problem was a tool problem. I tried every app and nothing stuck, so I built my own. It didn't cure procrastination but the act of building a focus tool while actively avoiding other work taught me something about how my brain operates under pressure.
